{"covered_dims": {"R1.5": 1.0, "RX.1": 0.7, "RX.2": 0.7, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 0.5, "RX.5": 0.7}}

Reordered the four paragraphs so the section moves problem setup → prior work → contribution → empirical results, without altering wording.

---

We consider stabilization of a partially observed linear time-invariant
plant under sensor scheduling. The dynamics follow x_{k+1} = A x_k +
B u_k + w_k, with w_k zero-mean Gaussian process noise of covariance Q.
At each step k the controller activates exactly one of m candidate
sensors, each yielding y_k^{(i)} = C_i x_k + v_k^{(i)} at communication
cost c_i. We assume (A, B) is stabilizable and that each pair (A, C_i)
is detectable, so that any single-sensor schedule admits a finite
steady-state Kalman filter.

Prior work on sensor scheduling has either solved the open-loop
selection problem via dynamic programming over the Riccati recursion
(Vitus et al., 2012) or relaxed it to a convex program over time-
averaged activation frequencies (Joshi and Boyd, 2009). Value-of-
information criteria from estimation theory have also been ported to
switched control settings (Krishnamurthy, 2016). These approaches
typically compute the schedule offline and assume the cost structure
is fixed a priori.

The principal contribution of this section is a closed-form one-step
lookahead rule, which we call regret-weighted EVPI, that selects the
sensor maximizing the expected reduction in LQG cost-to-go relative
to a cheapest-sensor default. We further establish, in Proposition 4,
that under bounded process noise the rule yields an exponentially
stable closed-loop with explicit decay rate rho < 1 depending only
on (A, B, Q) and the minimum sensor cost. The rule admits an
O(m n^2) per-step implementation and is fully state-feedback rather
than open-loop. It is this rule, defined in Eq. (14), together with
the stability certificate of Proposition 4, that produces the
quantitative gains tabulated below; without them the method collapses
to standard certainty-equivalence control.

On a four-tank benchmark (Johansson, 2000) and on a quadrotor
altitude-hold task with three candidate altimeters, our method
achieves a 23% reduction in mean tracking error against the round-
robin baseline and a 17% reduction against the greedy variance-
minimization rule of Mo et al. (2014), under matched communication
budgets. Closed-loop trajectories remained bounded across all 200
randomly initialized trials, and the empirical cost-to-go matched
the LQG lower bound to within 4% on the linearized four-tank model.
A representative disturbance-rejection trajectory is shown in
Figure 3, and Table 2 reports the per-budget breakdown.

---

Notes on the change:

- The original order was setup → prior work → results → contribution. Readers hit the empirical numbers before being told what method produced them, and the contribution paragraph then back-references those numbers ("the preceding paragraph"). Swapping the last two paragraphs puts the method statement adjacent to prior work (so the delta is visible in context) and immediately before the results that depend on it.
- One small phrase had to change to preserve referential coherence: "tabulated in the preceding paragraph" became "tabulated below", since the results paragraph now follows rather than precedes. No other wording was altered.
- Within-paragraph sentence order and all technical content are unchanged.

Caveats: I assumed the four paragraphs constitute a self-contained section and that no earlier section already states the contribution — if Section 1 or an abstract front-loads the contribution, you may prefer to keep the original ordering so the results land first and the rule is restated as a reminder. I also assumed "Figure 3" and "Table 2" are numbered globally rather than by section, so their references survive the reorder; worth a quick check against the surrounding manuscript.

rationale.R1.5: The delegation explicitly asks to restructure the section flow, making section-level ordering and narrative arc the load-bearing responsibility.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection should preserve uncertainty about unstated authorial intent or target venue conventions.
rationale.RX.2: The restructuring responsibility should avoid implying authority to strengthen claims beyond what the artifact supports.
rationale.RX.3: The request is limited to section flow and does not authorize rewriting, critique, or substantive research evaluation.
rationale.RX.4: Changes to flow could affect how readers interpret evidence and contributions, so downstream misrepresentation risks must be avoided.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should remain traceable to the delegation phrase and the artifact's section-level organization.
